title: Chapter 1: The Glass Table
content: "—which puts the Whitmore endowment at nearly twice what we projected for the spring gala."

My father's voice moved through the dining room like weather—inevitable, indifferent to whatever stood in its path. I sat in the third chair from the left, twelve seats arranged around a table of glass so thick it looked frozen, and I counted the seconds between his sentences the way other girls counted stars. Four hundred and twelve since I'd sat down. Approximately two thousand more until dismissal.

The candelabras threw silver claws across the ceiling. Between them, the fireplace roared with its unnatural green flame—my mother's aesthetic obsession, gas lines rigged to burn verdigris, the color of oxidation and old money. The lamb on my plate had gone cold. I could still smell it, though: rosemary and rendered fat, the char of the crust where the kitchen had seared it just right. I hadn't taken a second bite. Hunger was a language I'd stopped speaking at twelve.

Robert Vance did not eat either. He presided. That was the word—presided—though no one would say it aloud because Robert Vance's vocabulary was the only one that operated in this house. He sat at the head of the table in his charcoal suit, the silver at his temples the only concession to his fifty-three years, and he held court over the Whitmore Foundation donors like a man who had never once wondered whether the room would follow him. It always followed him. Rooms had been following Robert Vance since before I was born.

"The architecture committee approved the new library wing last Thursday," he continued, and his eyes—that particular shade of gray-green that I saw every morning in my own mirror—swept the table to ensure everyone was listening. They were. Robert Vance didn't repeat himself. "Catherine will be performing at the dedication."

Eight words. Eight words that rearranged the next three months of my life, and he delivered them the way he delivered everything: as fact. Not a question. Not a consideration. Just truth, laid down like stone.
Catherine will be performing.

I smiled. Small. Demure. The smile I'd been perfecting since I was six years old—the one that said *thank you, I'm honored* without any of the desperation that usually accompanied gratitude. My fingers curled under the tablecloth's embroidered edge, the rough thread catching beneath my nails, and I held the pose.
Across from me, my mother reached for her wine glass.

Margaret Vance—née Whitmore, though she'd surrendered the surname's power the moment she'd taken my father's—sat in the chair that mirrored mine on the table's other side, and she was dissolving. That was the only word for it. The way salt disappears in water, quiet and complete. Her third glass of the evening caught the candelabra light, and I watched the legs—the slow, articulate runs of burgundy sliding down the crystal—and I thought: *that's what surrender looks like under a microscope.*
Her hand trembled as she set the glass down. It trembled again reaching for the butter knife.
My fingers twitched under the tablecloth. A reflex. The muscle memory of reaching for someone you love, someone who is falling, someone who needs—
I did not reach.
I never reached.

I had learned, at nine years old, that my mother's flinching was not an invitation. It was a geography. A map of all the places my father's words had landed, and if I touched her — if I offered comfort even once — I would become a coordinate on that map too. So I sat. I sat with my spine at the precise angle Robert had corrected into me, and I watched my mother drown in her third glass of wine, and I let my empathy calcify into performance.
That was the trick. That was the whole trick. Feel nothing that shows. Show nothing that bleeds.

"Catherine." One of the Whitmores—Eleanor, I thought, the older one with the emerald brooch—tilted her head at me with the studied warmth of a woman who wrote checks to boarding schools and called it charity. "Your father tells us you've been preparing for a recital. Chopin, was it?"

"Liszt," I said, and let a faint color rise to my cheeks. Not too much. Just enough. "The Liebesträum. Number three." I paused—beat—then added, with the careful humility of a girl who knows her talent is a currency she didn't earn: "I'm not sure I'm ready. But Papa believes in me, so I try."

The Whitmores melted. I could see it happen—the slight softening of Eleanor's jaw, the way her companion leaned forward, the almost imperceptible nod of approval. They'd been handed the exact narrative they wanted: grateful daughter, doting father, the Vance legacy crystallizing into its next generation. I felt their satisfaction like a physical thing, warm and weightless, and I despised it with a heat that could have lit the green flame without gas.
Robert's gaze touched me. Brief. Surgical. Then he gave the faintest nod—acknowledgment of a kill well made—and returned to his monologue about the foundation's quarterly projections.
I reached for my water glass. The silver rim was cold against my lips.

"Smile, Catherine." My father's voice, pitched low enough that only I could hear. The words slid beneath his ongoing sentence about donor retention rates, a duet he conducted without pause. "The Whitmores are watching."
I smiled.
The Whitmores watched.

And underneath—somewhere beneath the rosemary and the silver chime of utensils and the green fire that made the whole room look like the inside of an emerald, underneath all of it—the rage turned over in my chest like something living. Not hot. Not the crude burn of anger that makes people shout and slam doors. This was the other kind. The pressure-cooker kind. The kind that calcifies into diamonds if you hold it long enough.
I held it. I was very, very good at holding it.

Dessert was panna cotta with blackberries, and I ate exactly three bites, spaced four minutes apart, because that was what Robert's nutritionist had prescribed and because the act of eating—chewing, swallowing, consuming—felt like a concession I wasn't willing to make more than three times. When the Whitmores' coffee cups were half-empty and the conversation had moved to property taxes, my father's gray-green eyes found me again.
"You're excused, Catherine."

Three words. Permission and dismissal braided into the same breath. I stood, chair legs silent on the Persian rug—another skill, another refinement, another way of making myself a thing that does not disturb the air around Robert Vance—and I crossed the dining room without looking back at my mother.
I didn't need to look. I could hear her glass hit the table a half-second after I left.

The portrait gallery ran the length of the hall between the dining room and the east wing. Fourteen paintings, each one a Vance, each one hung at the precise height my father had specified: center of the canvas at sixty-two inches, eye level for a man of his stature. Oil and varnish and gilt frames, and every pair of eyes painted with the same cold authority that Robert wore like a second skin.
I walked past ten of them without slowing. The eleventh made me stop.

Elias Vance. 1847–1912. Founder of Blackwood Academy. My great-great-grandfather, rendered in oils thick as blood, standing before the iron gates of the school with his hand resting on a cane and his expression suggesting he had never once in his life been uncertain about anything. The paint had cracked along his jawline—a fault line running through the founder's certainty—and someone should have had it restored. My father would never allow it. Cracks, to Robert, were evidence. Proof that the structure could hold under pressure.
I pressed my thumb against the gilt frame. The gold leaf was cool and slightly sharp where age had flaked it away, and I held my thumb there—one beat, two—until the thin edge bit into my skin.

He built that school on bones. I'd found the letters when I was thirteen, stuffed behind a false panel in the study bookshelf. Elias Vance hadn't founded Blackwood Academy out of philanthropy. He'd built it because a man with that much land and that few scruples needed a place to put the things he didn't want the world to see. The school came later—the facade, the mission statement, the centuries of Whitmores writing checks. The bones came first.

My mouth moved. A whisper, barely shaped, too quiet for even the portraits to hear. I wasn't sure what I was saying—or I was, and I chose not to know, because knowing would make it real and I wasn't ready for real yet.
I pulled my thumb away. A faint indentation in the gold, barely visible. My skin was unbroken.
The mask went back up. Seamless. Perfect as armor.
I walked the rest of the gallery without stopping.

My room was at the end of the east wing, past the linen closet and the stairwell that led to the servants' quarters—a holdover from the house's Victorian architecture that my father kept because it pleased him to maintain the fiction that the staff didn't use the main stairs. I closed my door. Locked it. Turned the lock twice, though the second turn was unnecessary, because the ritual of it—the double action, the confirmation—was the only kind of control I had in this house.

The jewelry box sat on my vanity. A stupid, ornate thing—rosewood with brass hinges and a ballerina that used to spin to a tinkling lullaby when you opened the lid. My mother's gift for my twelfth birthday, back when she still believed that giving me beautiful things could make me beautiful in return.

I opened it. The ballerina didn't spin anymore; I'd removed the mechanism two years ago. Inside: pearl earrings, a garnet pendant, a watch I never wore. The ordinary armory of a Vance daughter's presentation.

My fingers found the hidden compartment. A panel in the base, thinner than a playing card, released when you pressed the left corner and slid right. I'd built the mechanism myself with a pocket knife and a steady hand, working by candlelight the summer I was fifteen, because Robert didn't allow locks on anything he couldn't open and this—one small secret in a house full of his secrets—was the quietest rebellion I could manage.
Inside the compartment: a single black rose.

Dried. Broken. One petal had separated from the head and lay beside it like a shed tear. I'd pressed it flat when I first put it there, and the years had made it brittle as tissue paper, a fragile dark thing that should have crumbled but hadn't. Wouldn't. I'd made sure of that.

I couldn't remember anymore whether someone had given it to me or whether I'd taken it. Both options felt true. Both options felt like the kind of truth that would cost me something if I examined it too closely, so I didn't examine it. I held the rose between my thumb and forefinger—gentle, impossibly gentle, the way I never held anything else—and I let my face go still.
Not the mask. Something else. Something underneath the mask. A face I didn't have a name for yet.
This girl is a weapon, I thought. And she is choosing not to fire.
Yet.
I placed the rose back in its compartment. Sealed the panel. Returned the garnet pendant to its position above the hidden space. Closed the jewelry box.
I changed into my nightgown. I brushed my hair one hundred strokes, left side then right, because routine was architecture and architecture was survival. I turned off the light.
The ceiling was invisible in the dark, but I stared at it anyway—the blankness of it, the nothing—and I listened.

One floor down, the green flame still burned. I could feel it, the way you feel a sound you can't quite hear: a low hum in the architecture, the house breathing its verdigris fire into the rooms where my parents sat at the glass table that reflected everything and concealed nothing.
Their voices rose. Or rather—Robert's voice stayed exactly where it always was, at the altitude of facts, and my mother's voice rose to meet it and failed.
"—can't keep doing this, Robert, she's our *daughter*—"
"She is a Vance. That is what she does."
"I never agreed to—"

"You agreed to everything the moment you said yes." His voice didn't rise. It never rose. It simply leveled, the way a blade levels against a throat, precise and unemotional and absolutely lethal. "Margaret. You're drunk. Go to bed."
Silence.

The kind of silence that happens when someone stops arguing—not because they've been convinced, but because they've been reminded of the cost. The silence of surrender. The silence I'd been memorizing since I was old enough to listen.
I heard my mother's footsteps on the stairs. Unsteady. The fourth step groaned—she always missed it when she'd been drinking—and then the hallway, and then the distant click of their bedroom door.

My father's footsteps did not follow. He would stay downstairs, I knew. He would sit in the green firelight with the remaining wine and the silence of a house that obeyed him, and he would think about the fundraiser and the Whitmores and the dedication ceremony, and he would not think about his wife or his daughter because thinking about people—really thinking about them, as beings with interiors and needs and breaking points—was a capacity Robert Vance had either lost or never possessed.
I closed my eyes.

The green flame was still burning downstairs. I could see it behind my eyelids, wrong-colored and constant, and when I opened them again it was there in my window—a reflection climbing the dark glass from the floor below, wavering like a signal.
A signal nobody else could read.
But I could read it. I'd been reading it for years.
Wait, it said. Wait and hold and calcify and hold and wait, and one day the pressure will become something useful. One day the diamond will be complete. One day the rose will have a reason.

I lay still in the dark of my room, a girl made of composure and rage and a single dried flower she couldn't bear to throw away, and I listened to the silence my mother had left behind, and I made myself a promise I didn't yet have the vocabulary to speak:
*Soon.*
